coredata.dc:title: Multi-sensing function integrated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots as the platform toward multi-mode detection and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Jiao, Yuan
Zhang, Yuan
Li, Yong
Gao, Yifang
Lu, Wenjing
Liu, Yang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully utilized as a photoluminescent platform for multi-mode sensing of chromium(VI) [Cr(VI)], 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) and ascorbic acid (AA). The N-CDs with green fluorescent emission were synthesized via one-pot method using p-phenylenediamine and ammonia as precursors. The structure and optical properties were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. Cr(VI) and TNP were able to absorb the excitation band and emission band of N-CDs and decreased the photoluminescence of N-CDs from internal filtration effect (IFE). Besides, the construction of N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture can implement as a fluorescence platform for further detection of AA on the basis of the oxidation-reduction reactions between Cr(VI) and AA. Meanwhile, as-prepared N-CDs exhibit strong photostability, low toxicity and great biocompatibility. Moreover, the as-fabricated N-CDs have been smoothly achieved bioimaging in SMMC7721 cells owing to its low toxicity. It is prospective that N-CDs can be applied as a rapid multi-function sensing platform to biosensing and biomedical optical imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-sensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Chromium(VI)
2,4,6-Trinitrophenol
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP
Detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP
Mechanism of Cr(VI) and AA assay
Detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 , 21475080 and 21575084 ), Shanxi Scholarship Council. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of N-CDs. (b) AFM image of N-CDs.
(a) FTIR (b) XPS (c) C1s (d) N1s spectra of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (b) Excitation-emission matrix of N-CDs. (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates from the photoluminescence spectra of N-CDs.

(a) The quenching of photoluminescence of N-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) (0–11.5 μM), the inset is dependence of (F0–F)/F0 on the Cr(VI) concentrations (0.0375–3 μM). (b) PL emission spectra of N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture to various contents of AA (0–65 μM), the inset is the relationship between (F–F0)/F0 and concentration of AA (5–50 μM). (c) Photoluminescence spectra of N-CDs upon addition of TNP, the inset is a linear correlation of (F–F0)/F0 values versus TNP concentration in the range of 19–27 μM.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of (a) Cr(VI) and (b) TNP and photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs.

The confocal fluorescent microscopic images of (a1-c1) N-CDs, (a2-c2) N-CDs/Cr(VI), (a3-c3) N-CDs/Cr(VI)/AA in SMMC7721 cells, shooting conditions: bright field (left column), dark field (middle column), superimposed picture (right column).

The confocal fluorescent microscopic images of (a1-c1) N-CDs, (a2-c2) N-CDs/TNP in SMMC7721 cells, shooting conditions: bright field (left column), dark field (middle column), superimposed picture (right column).
Illustration for the synthetic procedure of the N-CDs and the application of N-CDs as a multi-function sensing platform.

head.ce:title: Multi-sensing function integrated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots as the platform toward multi-mode detection and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully utilized as a photoluminescent platform for multi-mode sensing of chromium(VI) [Cr(VI)], 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) and ascorbic acid (AA). The N-CDs with green fluorescent emission were synthesized via one-pot method using p-phenylenediamine and ammonia as precursors. The structure and optical properties were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. Cr(VI) and TNP were able to absorb the excitation band and emission band of N-CDs and decreased the photoluminescence of N-CDs from internal filtration effect (IFE). Besides, the construction of N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture can implement as a fluorescence platform for further detection of AA on the basis of the oxidation-reduction reactions between Cr(VI) and AA. Meanwhile, as-prepared N-CDs exhibit strong photostability, low toxicity and great biocompatibility. Moreover, the as-fabricated N-CDs have been smoothly achieved bioimaging in SMMC7721 cells owing to its low toxicity. It is prospective that N-CDs can be applied as a rapid multi-function sensing platform to biosensing and biomedical optical imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Multi-mode sensor has caught attention to analysts due to its integration of multiple functions and ease of operation [1]. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have played the important role in multi-mode sensing application from their high performance characteristics, such as tunable excitation/emission, strong photochemical stability, superior biocompatibility and lower cytotoxicity [2–5]. Surface engineering as the effective approach may introduce more active sites for CDs, they enable to cause different fluorescent responses to the analytes based on different principles, including static quenching [6–8], dynamic quenching [9–11], photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [12–15], inner filter effect (IFE) [16–19] and so on. Both static quenching and PET change the fluorescent signal by forming a compound between CDs and quencher, and dynamic quenching is the energy transfer or charge transfer among them. The IFE is attributed to the absorption bands of the quencher (absorber) partly overlapping the excitation and/or emission light of the CDs [20–23]. Compared with other quenching mechanisms, IFE does not involve any electronic or energy transfer process, nor does it require further complex modifications to the surface of CDs. Therefore, it is worth reasonable to construct an IFE-based fluorescent sensing platform to realize multi-functional sensing in biological system. (see Scheme 1 )

At present, a variety of single-mode sensing systems on the basis of carbon dots have been developed for individual analyte, such as metal ions [24], amino acid [25] and antibiotic [26] etc. For most of these systems, CD-based fluorescent sensors can only response to single analyte. In order to further enrich their detection function, some CDs have been doped as dual-mode sensors in response to two targets, Zheng et al. [27] reported the first application of CDs as a fluorescence probe for determination of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid. Up to data, dual-mode detection had been extended to real samples and cellular imaging [28–30]. Even multi-mode fluorescent sensing systems have also been involved for multitask purpose. Qu et al. [31] introduced dual-emission carbon dots with different modified ligands for determination of Fe3+, Bi3+ and Al3+ in common drugs. The prospective report was of interest because it improved the sensing efficiency of carbon dots and furnished a novel sensing mechanism, which could give the different sight for multi-mode sensing based on unitary carbon dots. Several researches employed CDs as the multi-sensing probe capable of detecting multiple analytes in real samples [32–35]. However, CDs of multi-sensing targets for bioimaging are challengeable. A dual-channel chemosensor based on bis(3-pyridylmethyl)amine -functionalized CDs was fabricated for selective determination of Cu(II), Ag(I) and glutathione in live cells, which inspired us to integrate CDs into functionalized platform toward multi-targets detection and bioimaging [36].

Recently, a multitude of papers have been reported on the application and preparation of carbon dots as fluorescent probes for detection of heavy metals and environmental analysis [37–39]. Chromium is a highly toxic heavy metal that can easily enter human cells and causes damage to internal organs such as the liver and kidneys [40]. The accumulation of chromium in the human body may induce gene mutation and lead to cancer [41]. Thus, the development of Cr(VI) analysis toward CDs multi-mode sensing is of important concern. Detection principles of Cr(VI) were mainly owing to the internal filtration effect [42,43]. It is better understanding that the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) are centered at 350 nm and 440 nm and can usually overlap with the excitation peak and/or emission peak of CDs [44]. Ascorbic acid (AA) is a most important vitamins which enables to improve immunity, prevent cancer and heart disease, protect teeth and gums [45,46]. Abnormal levels of AA can increase the risk of cancer and mental illness, which is usually detected based on its reducing properties [47,48]. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) not only causes serious environmental pollution, but human inadvertent intake or exposure to TNP may cause skin irritation, liver or kidney damage, purpura and aplastic anemia [49–52]. TNP has a broad absorption band and ease of overlap with the excitation peak of CDs, which may be further detected via establish CDs-based internal filter sensors [53–55]. Based on the above considerations, we try to develop a multi-mode sensing carbon dots platform from the internal filtering effect for monitoring of Cr(VI), TNP and AA.

In this work, multi-function sensing integrated a single N-CDs platform for determination of Cr(VI), TNP and AA. The green emissive N-CDs with an excitation dependent emission were conveniently fabricated by a one-pot hydrothermal route of p-phenylenediamine and ammonia as precursors. A fluorescence methodology to the detection of individual Cr(VI) and TNP was manufactured using the N-CDs as a multi-analytes sensor from internal filtration effect (IFE). The introduction of AA into N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture induces the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which could block IFE process and recover the fluorescence. Therefore, it can establish a multiple function sensing platform from the as-prepared N-CDs to detect Cr(VI), TNP and AA via internal filtration effect and oxidation-reduction effect. The multi-sensing system presented a good relationship to Cr(VI), TNP and AA, respectively. Moreover, the as-fabricated N-CDs exhibit great biocompatibility, low toxicity and strong photostability, which empowering N-CDs with great potential to monitor Cr(VI), TNP and AA in biosystem.

P-Phenylenediamine (p-PD, 98%) and ammonia (25%) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were bought from Solarbio (Beijing, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) from a MilliQ Plus system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Typically, 216 mg of p-PD and 500 μL of ammonia were dissolved in 50 mL of a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with 20 mL of ultrapure water, with heating at 200 °C for 8 h. The crude product was dialyzed against ultrapure water by dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off, MWCO: 500e1000 Da) for 3 days. The purple-red N-CDs were lyophilized to collect pure N-CDs product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs solution (0.25 mg/mL) were prepared by freeze-dried N-CDs. To assess the application of Cr(VI) assay, 10 μL of different contents of Cr(VI) were mixed to 2.0 mL of N-CDs solution, and photoluminescence spectra were recorded. AA was detected by the above N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture, and Cr(VI) concentration was 0.3 mM. Different contents of AA were mixed to N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture, and recovered photoluminescence spectra were examined. In the determination of TNP, 10 μL of different concentrations of TNP were added into 2.0 mL of N-CDs solution, and photoluminescence spectra were measured. The sample obtained by 2.0 mL N-CDs solution was used as the control sample. The photoluminescence excitation and emission of all the samples were recorded at 361 and 515 nm.

Human hepatic carcinoma SMMC7721 cells were incubated in DMEM supplied with 10% FBS, and the cells were seeded in the culture dish. Then, the N-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) were added into the wells, and the cells were incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, the cells were washed three times with fresh pH7.4 phosphate buffer solutions to remove the remaining N-CDs. The cells were incubated with 0.25 mg/mL N-CDs for 30 min as described above, followed by incubation with 0.20 mM Cr(VI), 0.20 mM Cr(VI)/5.0 mM AA and 0.10 mM TNP for 20 min at 37 °C under a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2, respectively. Cellular fluorescence images were recorded by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM).

The tap water samples were obtained from Taiyuan and Wutai (China) and the rain water sample was obtained from Wutai. The water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter.

Orange juices and Kiwi were bought from a local supermarket in Taiyuan (China), and used immediately after treatment. Orange juices were centrifuged at 10 °C in a refrigerated centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 5.0 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter and then diluted to an appropriate concentration in the detection range with ultrapure water. Kiwi was washed with ultrapure water and dried naturally in air at room temperature and accurately weighed and homogenized by using a commercial blender. After centrifugation at 10 °C in a refrigerated centrifuge at 3000 rpm for 20min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter.
Urine sample were collected from healthy humans in our laboratory. The normal fresh urine sample was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min.

The structure and morphology of N-CDsare characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM image (Fig. 1 a) indicates that N-CDs is uniform and monodisperse, with the average diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. S1a). The High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that a lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. Additionally, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. 1b) exhibits that the N-CDs has a narrow size distribution with an average height of approximately 3.2 nm, which is in agreement with the results of the TEM image.

To further analyze the chemical structure of N-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are performed. The FTIR analysis (Fig. 2 a) demonstrates that the surface of the N-CDs contained O–H and N–H (3200-3414 cm−1), CN (1627 cm−1), CC (1452 cm−1) and C–N (1255 cm−1). The full XPS spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 2b) shows C1s, N1s and O1s peaks are located at 282.0, 395.8 and 528.6 eV, respectively. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) reveals the signals of sp2 carbons (CC) at 284.7 eV, sp3 carbons (C–O/C–N) at 286.0 eV and carboxyl carbons (COOH) at 288.8 eV. The N1s band (Fig. 2d) consists of pyridine-N, amino-N and pyrrolic-N, which are assigned to binding energy 398.4 eV, 399.1 eV and 400.8 eV, respectively. The O1s band in Fig. S1b can be disassembled into double peaks at 531.82 eV for O–N/C–O–C and 533.25 eV for –CO. Based on the FTIR and XPS spectra, the N-CDs contain plenty of amino and carboxyl groups, which enables excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution of the N-CDs. The zeta potential (Fig. S2) of the N-CDs is 3.33 mV from amino groups on the surface, which is consistent with the corresponding XPS and FTIR results.

The remarkable optical properties of N-CDs are investigated by UV–Vis absorption, photoluminescent spectra. Owing to π→π* transition of the CC bond and the n →π* transition of the CO bond, the N-CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 305 nm (Fig. 3 a). Similar to most CDs in prior works, the as-prepared N-CDs solution (Fig. 3b) exhibit excitation wavelength dependent properties, and the maximum excitation and the maximum emission wavelengths are near 361 and 515 nm, respectively. To further probe the fluorescence emission of the N-CDs, chromaticity diagram (CIE) is explored. As shown in Fig. 3c, CIE coordinates can be calculated as (0.5948,0.4046), which demonstrates the feasibility of proposed N-CDs as a photoluminescent sensing platform. The QY of the N-CDs reaches 13.2% (Fig. S3). In addition, the N-CDs express good photostability for photoluminescent intensity remains invariant under continuously irradiated with 60 min (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the high ionic strength and different pH values (3–12) have negligible impacts for the PL intensity of N-CDs, which proves more latent application of N-CDs in biological sample analysis and bioimaging studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a showed that introducing Cr(VI) could bring an obvious fluorescence quenching of about 93% of the primaeval PL intensity. There was an excellent linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) and Cr(VI) concentration in the range from 0.0375 to 3 μM (Fig. 4a inset, y = 0.146x+0.005, R2 = 0.9970). The LOD was 22.5 nM.

To further investigate the selectivity for determination of Cr(VI), the effects of representative metal ions on the fluorescence of the N-CDs were examined. The results (Fig. S7) illustrated that the N-CDs probe possessed an excellent selectivity of Cr(VI) sensing.

Accompanied with the increase of AA concentration, the quenched photoluminescence of the N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture was gradually restored (Fig. 4b). The relative fluorescence intensity (F–F0)/F0 of N-CDs/Cr(VI) in the absence and presence of AA was linear with AA concentration in the range of 5–50 μM (Fig. 4b inset, y = 0.0946x+0.4952, R2 = 0.9996). The LOD was 3.2 μM.

In addition, to validate the selectivity of AA sensing, other reducing agents were investigated as control samples. Fig. S8 demonstrated that the reducing agents had negligible effect on the AA assay. As a result, the N-CDs/Cr(VI) mixture has the great potential for AA determination.

In Fig. 4c, with the increase in the concentration of TNP, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually decreased. The inset of Fig. 6a indicated that a good linear correlation was obtained over TNP concentration from 19 to 27 μM (Fig. 4c inset, y = 0.0119x + 0.5378, R2 = 0.9910). And the LOD was 3.69 μM.

To further investigate the selectivity of TNP sensing, other potential common substances were selected as control samples. The results (Fig. S9) indicated that the TNP-induced photoluminescence quenching of N-CDs was specific. In contrast, other substances had little influence on the PL intensity of the N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Cr(VI) and AA assay
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the photoluminescent titration experiment was monitored by N-CDs at its maximum excitation wavelength (361 nm). As shown in Fig. 5 a, the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) showed three broad bands at 260 nm, 350 nm and 450 nm and overlapped with the excitation and emission band of N-CDs. Therefore, the spectral overlap of Cr(VI) and N-CDs deduced that the photoluminescent quenching of N-CDs was attribute to IFE. Additionally, to further insight into the time-resolved decay data of the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the N-CDs was obtained in the absence and in the presence of Cr(VI) by time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC). The fluorescence decay curve (Fig. S10a) was well fitted by a triple exponential function, and the calculated average lifetimes were 5.00 ns (N-CDs) and 5.02 ns (N-CDs/Cr(VI)), respectively. The average life expectancy was almost unchanged ignores the interaction of the excited state in the presence of Cr(VI), which comfirmed that the Cr(VI)-induced photoluminescence quenching was strongly attributed to internal filter effect.

According to previous reports, Cr(VI) can be smoothly reduced to Cr(III) owing to the reducing property of AA, which made fluorescence of the system dramatically recovered. As a consequence, the constructed N-CDs/Cr(VI) sensing system has the potential for monitoring AA based on oxidation-reduction effect.

To further investigate the mechanism of the quenching process, UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime were measured. Fig. 5b showed that the characteristic absorption spectrum of TNP at 356 nm and the N-CDs showed excitation band centered at 365 nm and excitation band at 515 nm. Thus, the spectral overlap of the TNP and the N-CDs confirmed that the internal filter effect (IFE) may cause the photoluminescent quenching of N-CDs after adding TNP. As shown in Fig. S10b, there was no change in the lifetime of N-CDs before and after the addition of TNP, which further confirmed the IFE occurred between N-CDs and TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high sensitivity and selectivity, the proposed multi-sensing platform based on the N-CDs can be directly applied for detecting Cr(VI), AA and TNP in real samples. In order to evaluate the feasibility of the multi-sensing platform for the detection of Cr(VI), AA and TNP in real samples, quantitative analysis of Cr(VI) and TNP in the tap water (Taiyuan and Wutai) and rain water (Wutai) samples, and AA in human urine, kiwi and orange juice. To determine the recoveries of the multi-mode sensor in determination of Cr(VI), AA and TNP, different concentrations of Cr(VI), AA and TNP were added into the real samples, respectively.

As shown in Tables S1–3, the recoveries for the selective detection of Cr(VI) are 95.0–104.9% with the RSDs <2.6% (n = 6), AA are 96.4–103.4% with the RSDs <2.9% (n = 6), and TNP are 96.7–104.5% with the RSDs <2.9% (n = 6). Thus, the as-proposed multi-mode fluorescent sensing platform is promising in monitoring of the amount of Cr(VI), AA and TNP in real samples.

Furthermore, in order to validate the accuracy of the sensing method, an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was employed to measure Cr(VI) and a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was utilized to measure AA and TNP in real samples. As shown by Table S4, the results of ICP-MS in real samples are 189.53 (0.64 μM), 368.66 (1.25 μM) and 753.20 ng/mL (2.56 μM), respectively. These results demonstrated a satisfactory agreement between the results of the proposed sensing method in manuscript and ICP-MS, indicating that the N-CDs can be utilized as a fluorescence probe of the amount of Cr(VI) in real samples with accurate results. Meanwhile, Figs. S12 and S14 demonstrated the linear equation between the concentration of AA and TNP and integral area proposed by HPLC, respectively. The corresponding chromatograms of real samples are depicted in Figs. S12 and S15. The results of HPLC depicted in Tables S5 and S6 for AA and TNP are corresponding with our proposed sensing method. Thus, the as-proposed fluorescent platform is capable of on-line monitoring of the amount of Cr(VI), AA and TNP in real samples with accurate results.

The cytotoxicity of the N-CDs was assessed by the standard MTT assay exploiting human hepatic carcinoma SMMC7721 cells as the model. The cell viability of SMMC7721 cells (Fig. S11) was more than 85.6% after incubating with different contents of N-CDs (0–250 μg/mL) for 24 h, which demonstrated the low toxicity of N-CDs and inferred its potential application in cell imaging and biosensing.

To further probe the latent applications of N-CDs in living cells, green fluorescence was captured by LSCM. As shown in Fig. 6 , as expected, after treating SMMC7721 cells with the N-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) for 30 min at 37 °C, it is clear observed that significant green emission in the intracellular region. The green PL intensity quenched with the addition of Cr(VI), and the photoluminescence signal recovered when AA was continuously added. Also, Fig. 7 showed obvious fluorescence quenching after adding TNP in cells. These good results implied that the N-CDs was cell permeable and capable of detecting Cr(VI), TNP and AA in living cells.

In summary, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with an excitation dependent emission were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method using p-phenylenediamine and ammonia as precursors. The N-CDs were utilized as a multi-function sensing platform for determination of individual Cr(VI) and TNP from internal filtering effect (IFE). Moreover, the introduction of AA to the N-CDs/Cr(VI) system induced the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which enabled the fluorescence of the N-CDs/Cr(VI) system to recover. Therefore, a multi-mode strategy for monitoring Cr(VI), TNP and AA has been developed via IFE and oxidation-reduction effect. It is also noted that N-CDs exhibited lower toxicity and great biocompatibility, which endued N-CDs successfully achieve bioimaging in SMMC7721 cells. The multi-sensing platform from N-CDs is an effective approach for on-line sensitive monitoring of Cr(VI), TNP and AA in biomedical and biosensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087, 21475080 and 21575084), Shanxi Scholarship Council.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120653.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi sensing functions integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanisms
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, W. Liu, X. Sun, W. Pan, J. Wang, Multi sensing functions integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanisms, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 252 (2017) 544-553.

Adv. Mater.

ACS Nano

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Angew Chem. Int. Ed. Engl.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Truly fluorescent excitation-dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing
Adv. Mater.

Luminescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Sn(II) ions via quenching of the fluorescence of carbon nanodots
Microchimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N.A. Mohd Yazid, S.F. Chin, S.C. Pang, S.M. Ng, Detection of Sn(II) ions via quenching of the fluorescence of carbon nanodots, Microchimica Acta 180(1-2) (2012) 137-143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The synthesis of B, N-carbon dots by a combustion method and the application of fluorescence detection for Cu 2+
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.-C. Rong, K.-X. Zhang, Y.-R. Wang, X. Chen, The synthesis of B, N-carbon dots by a combustion method and the application of fluorescence detection for Cu 2+, Chinese Chemical Letters 28(5) (2017) 1119-1124.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent turn on sensing of Caffeine in food sample based on sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and optimization of process parameters through response surface methodology
